title: Search and Matching: The DMP Model
text:
A PDF version of these notes is available.
This note follows a lot from the note by Toshihiko Mukoyama.

title: Search and Matching: The DMP Model
text:
Beveridge curve condition
Consider ss. whre \(v\left(t\right)\equiv v,\forall t\) and \(u'\left(t\right)=0\): \[\begin{align*}
0 & =\sigma\left(1-u\right)-\lambda_{w}\left(\frac{v}{u}\right)u\\
\lambda_{w}\left(\frac{v}{u}\right)u & =\sigma\left(1-u\right)\\
u\left(\sigma+\lambda_{w}\left(\frac{v}{u}\right)\right) & =\sigma\\
u & =\frac{\sigma}{\sigma+\lambda_{w}\left(\frac{v}{u}\right)}
\end{align*}\]
This \(u=\frac{\sigma}{\sigma+\lambda_{w}\left(\frac{v}{u}\right)}\) is referred to as the Beveridge curve condition.

title: Search and Matching: The DMP Model
text:
Workers and Firms
The DMP builds on the unemp. dynamics and adds the endo. determination of agg. vacancy \(v\left(t\right)\). Firms create vacancies by trading off the current vacancy posting cost and future profit from the firm-worker match.
We assume that each vacancy hires one worker. Let \(w\left(t\right)\) be the eq. wage. The PV of being employed, in discrete-time setting w/ period length \(\Delta\) is \[\begin{equation}
W\left(t\right)=w\left(t\right)\Delta+\frac{1}{1+r\Delta}\left(\left(1-\sigma\Delta\right)W\left(t+\Delta\right)+\sigma\Delta U\left(t+\Delta\right)\right)\label{eq:W}
\end{equation}\] where \(U\left(t\right)\) is the value of being unemployed. \[U\left(t\right)=b\Delta+\frac{1}{1+r\Delta}\left(\lambda_{w}\left(\theta\left(t\right)\right)\Delta W\left(t+\Delta\right)+\left(1-\lambda_{w}\left(\theta\left(t\right)\right)\Delta\right)U\left(t+\Delta\right)\right)\]
Multiplying \(1+r\Delta\) on both sides of [eq:W] we get: \[\begin{align*}
\left(1+r\Delta\right)W\left(t\right) & =\left(1+r\Delta\right)w\left(t\right)\Delta+\left(\left(1-\sigma\Delta\right)W\left(t+\Delta\right)+\sigma\Delta U\left(t+\Delta\right)\right)\\
r\Delta W\left(t\right) & =\left(1+r\Delta\right)w\left(t\right)\Delta+\left(\left(1-\sigma\Delta\right)W\left(t+\Delta\right)+\sigma\Delta U\left(t+\Delta\right)\right)-W\left(t\right)\\
rW\left(t\right) & =\left(1+r\Delta\right)w\left(t\right)+\sigma U\left(t+\Delta\right)+\left(\frac{1}{\Delta}-\sigma\right)W\left(t+\Delta\right)-\frac{1}{\Delta}W\left(t\right)\\
rW\left(t\right) & =\left(1+r\Delta\right)w\left(t\right)+\sigma\left(U\left(t+\Delta\right)-W\left(t+\Delta\right)\right)+\frac{W\left(t+\Delta\right)-W\left(t\right)}{\Delta}
\end{align*}\]
so taking \(\Delta\to0\): \[rW\left(t\right)=w\left(t\right)+\sigma\left(U\left(t\right)-W\left(t\right)\right)+W'\left(t\right)\] Similarly for \(U\left(t\right)\): \[\begin{align*}
\left(1+r\Delta\right)U\left(t\right) & =\left(1+r\Delta\right)b\Delta+\left(\lambda_{w}\left(\theta\left(t\right)\right)\Delta W\left(t+\Delta\right)+\left(1-\lambda_{w}\left(\theta\left(t\right)\right)\Delta\right)U\left(t+\Delta\right)\right)\\
r\Delta U\left(t\right) & =\left(1+r\Delta\right)b\Delta+\left(\lambda_{w}\left(\theta\left(t\right)\right)\Delta W\left(t+\Delta\right)+\left(1-\lambda_{w}\left(\theta\left(t\right)\right)\Delta\right)U\left(t+\Delta\right)\right)-U\left(t\right)\\
rU\left(t\right) & =\left(1+r\Delta\right)b+\left(\lambda_{w}\left(\theta\left(t\right)\right)W\left(t+\Delta\right)-\lambda_{w}\left(\theta\left(t\right)\right)U\left(t+\Delta\right)\right)+\frac{U\left(t+\Delta\right)-U\left(t\right)}{\Delta}
\end{align*}\] so taking \(\Delta\to0\): \[rU\left(t\right)=b+\lambda_{w}\left(\theta\left(t\right)\right)\left(W\left(t\right)-U\left(t\right)\right)+U'\left(t\right)\]
Because of the one-firm to one-worker assumption, the firm is either matched or vacant, the value of a matched firm is: \[J\left(t\right)=p\left(t\right)\Delta-w\left(t\right)\Delta+\frac{1}{1+r\Delta}\left(\sigma\Delta V\left(t+\Delta\right)+\left(1-\sigma\Delta\right)J\left(t+\Delta\right)\right)\] where \(p\left(t\right)\Delta\)is the product produced by the match and \(V\left(t\right)\) is the value of vacancy, computed by: \[V\left(t\right)=-\kappa\Delta+\frac{1}{1+r\Delta}\left(\lambda_{f}\left(\theta\left(t\right)\right)\Delta J\left(t+\Delta\right)+\left(1-\lambda_{f}\left(\theta\left(t\right)\right)\Delta\right)V\left(t+\Delta\right)\right)\] where \(\kappa\Delta\) is the cost of posting a vacancy. It’s then easy to see that \[rJ\left(t\right)=p\left(t\right)-w\left(t\right)+\sigma\left(V\left(t\right)-J\left(t\right)\right)+J'\left(t\right)\] and \[\begin{equation}
rV\left(t\right)=-\kappa+\lambda_{f}\left(\theta\left(t\right)\right)\left(J\left(t\right)-V\left(t\right)\right)+V'\left(t\right)\label{eq:V}
\end{equation}\]
We assume that anyone can start a firm by posting a vacancy

if \(V\left(t\right)>0\), new entrants would keep posting vacancies until \(V\left(t\right)\) becomes \(0\). Additional vacancies would drop the value of \(V\left(t\right)\)because \(\lambda_{f}\left(\theta\left(t\right)\right)\) is a decreasing function of \(\theta\left(t\right)=\frac{v\left(t\right)}{u\left(t\right)}\). Therefore \(V\left(t\right)=0\) has to always hold, as long as \(v\left(t\right)>0\). 1
We call the condition \[V\left(t\right)=0\] the free-entry condition

One can formalize 1 as follows:
Let \(u\left(t\right)\) be the state var. at time \(t\), which evolves as \[u'=\sigma\left(1-u\right)-\lambda_{w}u\]
and \(v\left(t\right)\)be the control var. and \(v\ge0\).Given [eq:V] and taking \(u\left(t\right),J\left(t\right),V'\left(t\right)\)as given, we have \[V\left(t\right)=\frac{\lambda_{f}\left(\theta\left(t\right)\right)J\left(t\right)-\kappa+V'\left(t\right)}{r+\lambda_{f}\left(\theta\left(t\right)\right)}\] so \[\frac{\partial V}{\partial\lambda_{f}}=\frac{J\left(t\right)\left(r+\lambda_{f}\left(\theta\left(t\right)\right)\right)-\left(\lambda_{f}\left(\theta\left(t\right)\right)J\left(t\right)-\kappa+V'\left(t\right)\right)}{\left(r+\lambda_{f}\left(\theta\left(t\right)\right)\right)^{2}}\]
at ss., \(V'\left(t\right)=0\), so we have \[\begin{align*}
\frac{\partial V}{\partial\lambda_{f}} & =\frac{rJ\left(t\right)+\kappa-V'\left(t\right)}{\left(r+\lambda_{f}\left(\theta\left(t\right)\right)\right)^{2}}\\
& =\frac{rJ\left(t\right)+\kappa}{\left(r+\lambda_{f}\left(\theta\left(t\right)\right)\right)^{2}}>0
\end{align*}\]
Given the matching function, it’s easy to see that \(\lambda_{f}\left(\theta\right)=M\left(\frac{1}{\theta},1\right)\) is decreasing in \(\theta\): \[\frac{\partial\lambda_{f}}{\partial v}=\lambda_{f}'\left(\theta\right)\frac{1}{u}<0\]
so \[\frac{\partial V}{\partial v}=\frac{\partial V}{\partial\lambda_{f}}\frac{\partial\lambda_{f}}{\partial v}<0\]
Therefore, if \(V\left(t\right)>0\), then it follows that vacancy goes up and in turn pushing \(V\left(t\right)\to0\). If \(V\left(t\right)<0\), then nobody is willing to post a vacancy and in turn \(v\left(t\right)=0\). Finally if \(V\left(t\right)=0\), then entrants are indifferent and therefore \(\forall v\left(t\right)\ge0,s.t.V\left(v\left(t\right)\right)=0\) will work.

title: Search and Matching: The DMP Model
Search and Matching: The DMP Model
text:
Steady State Equilibrium
First, consider the ss. eq. where \(u\left(t\right)\) and \(v\left(t\right)\) are constant. With the conditions above we have \[\begin{align*}
rW & =w-\sigma\left(W-U\right)\\
rU & =b+\lambda_{w}\left(\theta\right)\left(W-U\right)\\
rJ & =p-w-\sigma\left(J-V\right)\\
rV & =-\kappa+\lambda_{f}\left(\theta\right)\left(J-V\right)\\
V & =0\\
\left(1-\gamma\right)\left(W-U\right) & =\gamma\left(J-V\right)
\end{align*}\]
in total 6 equations w/ 6 unknowns \(W,U,J,V,\theta,w\). We can then try to reduce this system to 1 to 1. It can be shown that we have \[(1-\gamma)(p-b)-\frac{r+\sigma+\gamma\lambda_{w}(\bar{\theta})}{\lambda_{f}(\bar{\theta})}\kappa=0.\] This condition is then used to solve for \(\theta\) and is often referred to as the job creation condition.
For simple math, we have \[\begin{align*}
rV & =-\kappa+\lambda_{f}\left(\theta\right)\left(J-V\right)\\
0 & =-\kappa+\lambda_{f}\left(\theta\right)\left(J-0\right)\\
J & =\frac{\kappa}{\lambda_{f}\left(\theta\right)}
\end{align*}\] Given \[\begin{align*}
rJ & =p-w-\sigma\left(J-V\right)\\
rJ & =p-w-\sigma J\\
J & =\frac{p-w}{r+\sigma}
\end{align*}\] we have the free-entry condition on firm side: \[\frac{\kappa}{\lambda_{f}\left(\theta\right)}=\frac{p-w}{r+\sigma}\]
Given \[\begin{align*}
rW & =w-\sigma\left(W-U\right)\\
rU & =b+\lambda_{w}\left(\theta\right)\left(W-U\right)\\
r\left(W-U\right) & =w-b-\left(\sigma+\lambda_{w}\left(\theta\right)\right)\left(W-U\right)\\
\left(r+\sigma+\lambda_{w}\left(\theta\right)\right)\left(W-U\right) & =w-b\\
W-U & =\frac{w-b}{r+\sigma+\lambda_{w}\left(\theta\right)}
\end{align*}\]
so given \(\left(1-\gamma\right)\left(W-U\right)=\gamma\left(J-V\right)\), we have \[\begin{align*}
\left(1-\gamma\right)\frac{w-b}{r+\sigma+\lambda_{w}\left(\theta\right)} & =\gamma\frac{\kappa}{\lambda_{f}\left(\theta\right)}\\
w-b & =\frac{\gamma}{1-\gamma}\frac{\kappa}{\lambda_{f}\left(\theta\right)}\left(r+\sigma+\lambda_{w}\left(\theta\right)\right)\\
p-w & =p-\left[\frac{\gamma}{1-\gamma}\frac{\kappa}{\lambda_{f}\left(\theta\right)}\left(r+\sigma+\lambda_{w}\left(\theta\right)\right)+b\right]\\
\left(r+\sigma\right)\frac{\kappa}{\lambda_{f}\left(\theta\right)} & =p-\left[\frac{\gamma}{1-\gamma}\frac{\kappa}{\lambda_{f}\left(\theta\right)}\left(r+\sigma+\lambda_{w}\left(\theta\right)\right)+b\right]\\
\left(r+\sigma\right)\kappa & =\lambda_{f}\left(\theta\right)\left(p-b\right)-\frac{\gamma\kappa}{1-\gamma}\left(r+\sigma+\lambda_{w}\left(\theta\right)\right)\\
\left(r+\sigma\right)\kappa+\frac{\gamma\kappa}{1-\gamma}\left(r+\sigma+\lambda_{w}\left(\theta\right)\right) & =\lambda_{f}\left(\theta\right)\left(p-b\right)\\
\left(1-\gamma\right)\left(r+\sigma\right)\kappa+\gamma\kappa\left(r+\sigma+\lambda_{w}\left(\theta\right)\right) & =\left(1-\gamma\right)\left(p-b\right)\lambda_{f}\left(\theta\right)\\
\left(r+\sigma\right)\kappa+\gamma\kappa\lambda_{w}\left(\theta\right) & =\left(1-\gamma\right)\left(p-b\right)\lambda_{f}\left(\theta\right)\\
\frac{r+\sigma+\gamma\lambda_{w}\left(\theta\right)}{\lambda_{f}\left(\theta\right)}\kappa & =\left(1-\gamma\right)\left(p-b\right)
\end{align*}\] which is exactly the job creation condition. To explain this: \[\underbrace{\frac{r+\sigma+\gamma\lambda_{w}\left(\theta\right)}{\lambda_{f}\left(\theta\right)}}_{\text{annuitized job creation cost}}\kappa=\underbrace{\left(1-\gamma\right)\left(p-b\right)}_{\text{firm's share of match surplus}}\]

title: Search and Matching: The DMP Model
text:
Transition Dynamics
Now let’s consider the transition dynamics by not imposing ss. conditions. Collect: \[\begin{align*}
rW\left(t\right) & =w\left(t\right)+\sigma\left(U\left(t\right)-W\left(t\right)\right)+W'\left(t\right)\\
rU\left(t\right) & =b+\lambda_{w}\left(\theta\left(t\right)\right)\left(W\left(t\right)-U\left(t\right)\right)+U'\left(t\right)\\
rJ\left(t\right) & =p\left(t\right)-w\left(t\right)+\sigma\left(V\left(t\right)-J\left(t\right)\right)+J'\left(t\right)\\
rV\left(t\right) & =-\kappa+\lambda_{f}\left(\theta\left(t\right)\right)\left(J\left(t\right)-V\left(t\right)\right)+V'\left(t\right)\\
\left(1-\gamma\right)\left(\tilde{W}\left(w,t\right)-U\left(t\right)\right) & =\gamma\left(\tilde{J}\left(w,t\right)-V\left(t\right)\right)\\
V\left(t\right) & =0
\end{align*}\] so \[\begin{align*}
0 & =-\kappa+\lambda_{f}\left(\theta\left(t\right)\right)\left(J\left(t\right)-0\right)\\
J\left(t\right) & =\frac{\kappa}{\lambda_{f}\left(\theta\left(t\right)\right)}
\end{align*}\] and \[\begin{align*}
J'\left(t\right) & =rJ\left(t\right)-\left[p\left(t\right)-w\left(t\right)+\sigma\left(V\left(t\right)-J\left(t\right)\right)\right]\\
& =\left(r+\sigma\right)J\left(t\right)-\left[p\left(t\right)-w\left(t\right)\right]\\
& =\left(r+\sigma\right)\frac{\kappa}{\lambda_{f}\left(\theta\left(t\right)\right)}-p\left(t\right)+w\left(t\right)
\end{align*}\] also given \(\left(1-\gamma\right)\left(W\left(t\right)-U\left(t\right)\right)=\gamma\left(J\left(t\right)-V\left(t\right)\right)\) holds, we have that \[\begin{align*}
\left(1-\gamma\right)\left(W'\left(t\right)-U'\left(t\right)\right) & =\gamma\left(J'\left(t\right)-V'\left(t\right)\right)\\
\left(1-\gamma\right)\left(W'\left(t\right)-U'\left(t\right)\right) & =\gamma J'\left(t\right)
\end{align*}\] and \[\begin{align*}
W'\left(t\right)-U'\left(t\right) & =rW\left(t\right)-\left[w\left(t\right)+\sigma\left(U\left(t\right)-W\left(t\right)\right)\right]-rU\left(t\right)+b+\lambda_{w}\left(\theta\left(t\right)\right)\left(W\left(t\right)-U\left(t\right)\right)\\
& =\left(r+\lambda_{w}\left(\theta\left(t\right)\right)+\sigma\right)\left(W\left(t\right)-U\left(t\right)\right)-w\left(t\right)+b
\end{align*}\] so \[\begin{align*}
\frac{\gamma J'\left(t\right)}{1-\gamma} & =\left(r+\lambda_{w}\left(\theta\left(t\right)\right)+\sigma\right)\frac{\gamma}{1-\gamma}\frac{\kappa}{\lambda_{f}\left(\theta\left(t\right)\right)}-w\left(t\right)+b\\
\frac{\gamma J'\left(t\right)}{1-\gamma}+J'\left(t\right) & =\left[\left(r+\lambda_{w}\left(\theta\left(t\right)\right)+\sigma\right)\frac{\gamma}{1-\gamma}\frac{\kappa}{\lambda_{f}\left(\theta\left(t\right)\right)}-w\left(t\right)+b\right]+\left[\left(r+\sigma\right)\frac{\kappa}{\lambda_{f}\left(\theta\left(t\right)\right)}-p\left(t\right)+w\left(t\right)\right]\\
\frac{J'\left(t\right)}{1-\gamma} & =\frac{r+\sigma+\gamma\lambda_{w}\left(\theta\left(t\right)\right)}{1-\gamma}\frac{\kappa}{\lambda_{f}\left(\theta\left(t\right)\right)}+b-p\left(t\right)\\
J'\left(t\right) & =\frac{r+\sigma+\gamma\lambda_{w}\left(\theta\left(t\right)\right)}{\lambda_{f}\left(\theta\left(t\right)\right)}\kappa-\left(1-\gamma\right)\left[p\left(t\right)-b\right]
\end{align*}\] I’ve been checking this and it seems that Toshihiko’s note is having the wrong sign here. Anyway let’s move on. Given \(J\left(t\right)=\frac{\kappa}{\lambda_{f}\left(\theta\left(t\right)\right)}\) we have \[\begin{align*}
J\left(t\right) & =\frac{\kappa}{\lambda_{f}\left(\theta\left(t\right)\right)}\\
J'\left(t\right) & =\frac{-\lambda_{f}'\left(\theta\left(t\right)\right)\theta'\left(t\right)}{\left[\lambda_{f}\left(\theta\left(t\right)\right)\right]^{2}}\\
& =\frac{\kappa}{\lambda_{f}\left(\theta\left(t\right)\right)}\underbrace{\left(-\frac{\lambda_{f}'\left(\theta\left(t\right)\right)\theta\left(t\right)}{\lambda_{f}\left(\theta\left(t\right)\right)}\right)}_{\equiv\eta\left(\theta\left(t\right)\right)}\frac{\theta'\left(t\right)}{\theta\left(t\right)}\\
& =J\left(t\right)\eta\left(\theta\left(t\right)\right)\frac{\theta'\left(t\right)}{\theta\left(t\right)}
\end{align*}\] where \(\eta\left(\theta\left(t\right)\right)=-\frac{\lambda_{f}'\left(\theta\left(t\right)\right)\theta\left(t\right)}{\lambda_{f}\left(\theta\left(t\right)\right)}>0\) is the elasticity of matching function. So if \(\theta\left(t\right)>\theta\) where \(\theta\) is the ss. value of the tightness, we have \(J'\left(t\right)>0\) so that \(\theta'\left(t\right)>0\), similarly if \(\theta\left(t\right)<\theta\), we have \(\theta'\left(t\right)<0\) . So that the only case where \(\theta\left(t\right)\) does converge is that \(\theta\left(t\right)\equiv\theta\), then it’s easy to see that \(J'\left(t\right)=0\) and \(J\left(t\right)\) is fixed too.
Therefore, we can say that job-creation condition holds even not in ss. So given \[\begin{align*}
\theta & =\frac{v\left(t\right)}{u\left(t\right)}\\
\frac{r+\sigma+\gamma\lambda_{w}\left(\theta\right)}{\lambda_{f}\left(\theta\right)}\kappa & =\left(1-\gamma\right)\left(p-b\right)\\
u'\left(t\right) & =\sigma\left(1-u\left(t\right)\right)-\lambda_{w}\left(\theta\left(t\right)\right)u\left(t\right)
\end{align*}\] one can pin down the dynamics of \(\left(v\left(t\right),u\left(t\right),\theta\right)\). Now let’s consider the case where \(u\left(t\right)>u\), where \(u\) is given by the Beveridge curve condition: \[u=\frac{\sigma}{\sigma+\lambda_{w}\left(\frac{v}{u}\right)}\] then we have \(u'\left(t\right)<0\), similarly we have \(u'\left(t\right)>0\) when \(u\left(t\right)<u\), so it’s easy to establish that \(u\left(t\right)\to u\) monotonically. Given \(v=\theta u\), the dynamic of \(v'\left(t\right)\) is similar.

title: Research
text:
October 2025 · paper


Abstract

Why do some sovereigns pay high spreads despite moderate debt? I build a quantitative default model where lenders overweight the dispersion of policy realizations (policy-randomness overestimation, PRO). Within a single pricing operator, PRO pivots price/spread schedules around a state-contingent threshold: the sovereign deleverages while defaulting at higher debt, raising average spreads and producing a “stability illusion” (lower volatility with higher premia). Policy and information extensions show limited power of fiscal transfers, persistence from negativity-biased learning, and welfare gains from transparency. Event-style hooks (e.g., Argentina’s inflation misreporting) align with the model’s pivot, threshold, and decoupling predictions. A rational-inattention microfoundation based on a mental-model-plus-residual representation endogenizes the tail weight within the same pricing operator.

Recipient of the 2024 Peking University Outstanding Undergraduate Research Project (funded).

title: Research

Abstract

Why do some sovereigns pay high spreads despite moderate debt? I build a quantitative default model where lenders overweight the dispersion of policy realizations (policy-randomness overestimation, PRO). Within a single pricing operator, PRO pivots price/spread schedules around a state-contingent threshold: the sovereign deleverages while defaulting at higher debt, raising average spreads and producing a “stability illusion” (lower volatility with higher premia). Policy and information extensions show limited power of fiscal transfers, persistence from negativity-biased learning, and welfare gains from transparency. Event-style hooks (e.g., Argentina’s inflation misreporting) align with the model’s pivot, threshold, and decoupling predictions. A rational-inattention microfoundation based on a mental-model-plus-residual representation endogenizes the tail weight within the same pricing operator.

title: Chen Gao (高琛)
text:
Welcome!
I am a senior undergraduate at the National School of Development, Peking University.
My research interests lie in macroeconomics, monetary economics, and computational methods, with a particular focus on heterogeneity. I enjoy combining structural modeling with data-driven approaches, and am especially interested in applying machine learning and deep learning to solve and estimate rich heterogeneous-agent models.
You can reach me at chengao0716 at gmail dot com.
